Parliamentary constituency in the United Kingdom, 1974 onwards

**Hartlepool** [/ˈhɑːrtlɪpuːl/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*HART-lih-pool*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") is a [borough constituency](/wiki/Borough_constituency "Borough constituency") represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom")[[a]](#cite_note-2) by [Jonathan Brash](/wiki/Jonathan_Brash "Jonathan Brash") of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") from 2024. The constituency covers the town of [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool "Hartlepool") plus nearby settlements.

Labour won every contest for the seat since the first at the [February 1974 election](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election") (and mostly won the predecessor constituency of [The Hartlepools](/wiki/The_Hartlepools_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "The Hartlepools (UK Parliament constituency)") from the [1945 election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") onward) until [Jill Mortimer](/wiki/Jill_Mortimer "Jill Mortimer") won the [2021 by-election](/wiki/2021_Hartlepool_by-election "2021 Hartlepool by-election"), becoming the first Conservative MP to represent Hartlepool since [1959](/wiki/1959_United_Kingdom_general_election "1959 United Kingdom general election"). However, she lost her seat back to Labour three years later, falling to third place.

## Constituency profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Constituency profile")]

The Hartlepool constituency is located in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") and is coterminous with the [Borough of Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"). It covers the large town of [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool "Hartlepool") and the villages to its west, including part of the new [garden village](/wiki/Garden_village "Garden village") of [Wynyard](/wiki/Wynyard,_County_Durham "Wynyard, County Durham"). Hartlepool is an industrial [port town](/wiki/Port_town "Port town") on [England](/wiki/England "England")'s [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") coast. The town was heavily involved in the shipping of coal from the nearby mining areas, but went into decline with the closure of industry and coal mining in the late 20th century. Today, the town experiences high levels of deprivation, with most parts falling within the top 10% most-deprived areas in England.[[2]](#cite_note-3) The average house price in Hartlepool is less than half the national average.[[3]](#cite_note-EC-4)

In general, residents of Hartlepool have low levels of education and income.[[3]](#cite_note-EC-4) A high proportion of residents work in the health and manufacturing industries and few work in professional fields.[[4]](#cite_note-5) [White people](/wiki/White_people_in_the_UK "White people in the UK") made up 96% of the population at the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_UK_Census "2021 UK Census").[[3]](#cite_note-EC-4) At [the local borough council](/wiki/Hartlepool_Borough_Council "Hartlepool Borough Council"), most of the town is represented by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") with some [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") elected in the rural west. Voters in Hartlepool overwhelmingly supported leaving the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in the [2016 referendum](/wiki/Brexit_referendum "Brexit referendum"); an estimated 70% voted in favour of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), making Hartlepool one of the top 25 most Brexit-supporting constituencies out of 650 nationwide.[[3]](#cite_note-EC-4)

## Boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boundaries")]

### 1974–1983

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1974–1983")]

The County Borough of Hartlepool.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Before 1974 the seat was known as [The Hartlepools](/wiki/The_Hartlepools "The Hartlepools") (reflecting the representation of both old Hartlepool and [West Hartlepool](/wiki/West_Hartlepool "West Hartlepool")). The name was changed following the merger in 1967 of the County Borough of West Hartlepool and the Municipal Borough of Hartlepool to form the County Borough of Hartlepool.

### 1983–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1983–present")]

The Borough of Hartlepool.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

*As a result of major local government boundary changes in 1974 arising from the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), the Borough of Hartlepool was incorporated into the new county of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland_(county) "Cleveland (county)"). As a consequence, a small part of the pre-1983 [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency was added to the seat.*

The [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies") left the boundaries unchanged.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

The seat is currently coterminous with the [borough of Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool_(borough) "Hartlepool (borough)"), which has close to the average population for a UK parliamentary constituency. The seat includes the town of Hartlepool itself and the nearby villages of [Hart](/wiki/Hart,_County_Durham "Hart, County Durham"), [Elwick](/wiki/Elwick,_County_Durham "Elwick, County Durham"), [Greatham](/wiki/Greatham,_County_Durham "Greatham, County Durham"), [Newton Bewley](/wiki/Newton_Bewley "Newton Bewley") and [Dalton Piercy](/wiki/Dalton_Piercy "Dalton Piercy").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Special:EditPage/Hartlepool (UK Parliament constituency)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Hartlepool" UK Parliament constituency](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Hartlepool%22+UK+Parliament+constituency&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The constituency had previously substantially been in the [constituency of The Hartlepools](/wiki/Constituency_of_The_Hartlepools "Constituency of The Hartlepools"). It became the **constituency of Hartlepool in 1974**.

Hartlepool was a [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") constituency from its creation until 2021, although its predecessor did have Conservative MPs both in the early 1960s and during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). At the [1992 general election](/wiki/1992_United_Kingdom_general_election "1992 United Kingdom general election"), [Ted Leadbitter](/wiki/Ted_Leadbitter "Ted Leadbitter") stood down and was succeeded by the former Labour Director of Communications [Peter Mandelson](/wiki/Peter_Mandelson "Peter Mandelson"). Mandelson's pivotal role in the reshaping of the Labour Party into [New Labour](/wiki/New_Labour "New Labour") attracted much attention, and he became a prominent target.

During the first term of the Labour government led by [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair"), Mandelson was twice appointed to the [Cabinet](/wiki/First_Blair_ministry "First Blair ministry") and twice forced to resign amid minor but controversial scandals. At the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") there was a notable contest when [Arthur Scargill](/wiki/Arthur_Scargill "Arthur Scargill"), former leader of the [National Union of Mineworkers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Mineworkers_(Great_Britain) "National Union of Mineworkers (Great Britain)") and the leader of the [Socialist Labour Party](/wiki/Socialist_Labour_Party_(UK) "Socialist Labour Party (UK)"), stood for election in the hope of exploiting uneasiness about New Labour in "traditional" [Labour heartlands](/wiki/Red_wall_(British_politics) "Red wall (British politics)"). In the event, Mandelson retained his seat, while Scargill polled only 912 votes. Mandelson shocked many with a triumphalist victory speech in which he declared "They underestimated Hartlepool, and they underestimated me, because I am a fighter and not a quitter!".[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The following year, the town's first [direct Mayoral election](/wiki/Mayor_of_Hartlepool "Mayor of Hartlepool") generated surprise when the mascot of [Hartlepool United F.C.](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C."), [H'Angus the Monkey](/wiki/H%27Angus_the_Monkey "H'Angus the Monkey") (real name [Stuart Drummond](/wiki/Stuart_Drummond "Stuart Drummond")) was elected on a platform that included free bananas for schoolchildren.

Mandelson resigned as MP for Hartlepool when he was appointed as a [European commissioner](/wiki/European_commissioner "European commissioner") in the summer of 2004. This triggered a by-election that took place on 30 September. The [Hartlepool by-election](/wiki/2004_Hartlepool_by-election "2004 Hartlepool by-election") – the last held prior to the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") – saw [Iain Wright](/wiki/Iain_Wright "Iain Wright") retain the seat for Labour with a majority of 2,033 votes. That by-election marked the first time that the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") had ever finished in third place at a by-election.

The Labour Party has continued to hold the seat since the by-election, with a dwindling majority and falling share of the vote, and at the three most recent general elections, three parties have finished in second place: the Liberal Democrats in 2005 (following their strong performance at the by-election the previous year), the Conservative Party in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), and UKIP, going one better than its by-election showing, in [2015](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election").

In May 2010, the Conservatives gained their largest percentage vote increase in the country in Hartlepool, reducing the Labour majority to just over 5,500, whilst in 2015, UKIP recorded their eleventh-highest vote share in the United Kingdom, taking 28% and reducing the Labour majority to just over 3,000 votes.

## 2010 general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010 general election")]

Both the 2010 and 2015 general elections (in addition to several local elections) took place against the backdrop of concerns regarding the potential closure of [Hartlepool and Stockton hospitals](/wiki/North_Tees_and_Hartlepool_NHS_Foundation_Trust "North Tees and Hartlepool NHS Foundation Trust") and their replacement with a new "super hospital" in out of town [Wynyard](/wiki/Wynyard,_County_Durham "Wynyard, County Durham"). This precipitated the closure of several departments, and the removal of services from Hartlepool. The move was initially supported by Hartlepool MP Iain Wright, and opposed by Stockton South candidate James Wharton at the 2010 general election. A substantial protest group was formed opposing Wynyard, and calling on services to remain at Hartlepool,[[11]](#cite_note-12) backed by a campaign by the *Hartlepool Mail*, a local newspaper.

Following the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), the [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") announced it would scrap the Wynyard proposals, although no guarantees were made regarding the future of Hartlepool hospital.[[12]](#cite_note-13) This issue continued to dominate politics in Hartlepool at both general elections and local council elections, which dented support for Iain Wright and Labour, who had backed the Wynyard plans, whilst many independent candidates gained traction.

At the 2010 general election, the Conservative Party approached Alan Wright, a regional broadcaster for the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") and columnist for the *Hartlepool Mail*, to stand as its candidate,[[13]](#cite_note-14) despite his lack of political and campaigning experience, hoping that his high-profile would help. It was also noted that the similarity of his name to that of the town's MP, and the fact he would feature above him on the ballot paper, might result in additional votes.[[14]](#cite_note-15) The Conservative Party gained a swing of 16.7%, the largest in the country, taking second place from the Liberal Democrats, and garnering it a vote share far exceeding their traditional local support.

## 2015 general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2015 general election")]

Sitting MP Iain Wright was the only candidate from 2010 to remain on the much-extended ballot paper in 2015, in which the three main parties faced competition from [UKIP](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party"), the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales"), and three [independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") candidates, each standing primarily on healthcare-related platforms.

Popular local taxi driver and charity fundraiser Stephen Picton put himself forward as the voice of the hospital campaigners,[[15]](#cite_note-16) although this was challenged by the last-minute candidacy of Sandra Allison, who stood under the banner of 'Your Vote Could Save Our Hospital'.[[16]](#cite_note-17) John Hobbs, an 80 year old autism campaigner stood under the tagline 'Tell it like it is'.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

UKIP earmarked Hartlepool as a potential gain, and the seat became one of its top ten national targets as well as its main target in the north-east, attracting significant party funding, visits from leader [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), and the regional party conference.[[18]](#cite_note-19) It selected Philip Broughton, a former Stockton Conservative Councillor and wrestling entrepreneur, as its candidate.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The Conservative Party selected public affairs consultant and competitive swimmer, Richard Royal, as its candidate.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

The Conservative Party's national '40/40 strategy' meant that much of its regional resources were directed towards the marginal seats of [Stockton South](/wiki/Stockton_South "Stockton South") and [Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland](/wiki/Middlesbrough_South_and_East_Cleveland "Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland"), forcing candidates to campaign outside of their selected seats.[[21]](#cite_note-22) The Green Party selected local member Michael Holt, who had been arrested the previous year for obstructing a police officer at a protest in London, although charges were subsequently dropped.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Just days before the nomination deadline, the Liberal Democrats selected [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington")-based Hilary Allen as its candidate.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

On [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day "Valentine's Day") in 2015, a 'We Love Our Hospital' rally was organised by Save Our Hospital and the Teesside Peoples' Assembly Against Austerity, attracting large crowds in Hartlepool town centre. Candidates Iain Wright, Richard Royal, Philip Broughton, Stephen Picton and Michael Holt each gave speeches alongside other selected speakers. It was reported that Wright was booed and heckled by the crowd.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)

One day prior to the general election, local football club [Hartlepool United F.C.](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") took the unprecedented step of openly criticising Wright, and seemingly encouraging fans to support either Royal or Broughton, both of whom had met the club's leadership and shown support for its interests.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The club had been under pressure, facing relegation and had an ongoing land dispute with the [Labour council](/wiki/Hartlepool_Borough_Council "Hartlepool Borough Council").

Throughout the campaign, both Phillip Broughton and Richard Royal sought to portray themselves as the only viable alternative to Iain Wright, with Broughton distributing leaflets claiming that the Hartlepool election was a 'two horse race',[[27]](#cite_note-28) and Royal referring to the close 2010 result, with his slogan "Wright for your town? Wrong for your future. Turn Hartlepool Royal Blue".[[28]](#cite_note-29) As a result, much of the anti Labour vote was split, with UKIP and the Conservatives gaining a combined 48.9% compared to Iain Wright's 35.6%, but neither taking enough votes individually to defeat Labour. At one point during election night itself, the vote looked so close that a recount was reported to be due,[[29]](#cite_note-30) but this proved to be unnecessary after the inclusion of postal votes.

## 2017 general election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2017 general election")]

Following the 2015 general election result, Hartlepool became the 35th most vulnerable Labour seat in the country.[[30]](#cite_note-31) At the [EU referendum in 2016](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") Hartlepool voted to 'Leave' by 69.5%, making it one of the highest Leave-voting Labour-held seats in the UK.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Despite this intense [Euroscepticism](/wiki/Euroscepticism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Euroscepticism in the United Kingdom") in the area making it perceived as a vulnerable seat for Labour, at the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") Labour's new candidate [Mike Hill](/wiki/Mike_Hill_(British_politician) "Mike Hill (British politician)") retained the seat, with UKIP's vote falling by 17 points and Labour's rising by 17 points. This gave Labour their biggest total vote and popular vote majority in Hartlepool since [2001](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election").

Following an allegation of sexual assault made against him in September 2019, Hill sat as an Independent. However, three weeks later, the allegation was withdrawn, and he had the Labour [whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)") restored.

## 2021 by-election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2021 by-election")]

Main article: [2021 Hartlepool by-election](/wiki/2021_Hartlepool_by-election "2021 Hartlepool by-election")

On 16 March 2021 Mike Hill resigned as the MP for Hartlepool, triggering a [by-election](/wiki/2021_Hartlepool_by-election "2021 Hartlepool by-election").[[32]](#cite_note-resignation-33) The election was won by [Jill Mortimer](/wiki/Jill_Mortimer "Jill Mortimer") of the Conservative Party. It was the first time the Conservatives had held the seat.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hartlepool_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** As with all constituencies, Hartlepool elects one Member of Parliament (MP) by the [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") system of election at least every five years.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-38)** Not the [former Deputy Prime Minister from 1997 to 2007](/wiki/John_Prescott "John Prescott")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-Walker_39-0)** Walker was a member of and endorsed by the [Northern Independence Party](/wiki/Northern_Independence_Party "Northern Independence Party"), but appeared on the ballot as an Independent due to that party not being registered with the Electoral Commission.
